g.data-narration: Welcome to Process Synchronization - the foundation for managing concurrent access to shared resources in modern computing systems.
Process synchronization coordinates multiple processes accessing shared resources to prevent race conditions and ensure correct system behavior.
g.text: Managing concurrent access to shared resources in multi-process/thread environments.
Prevents race conditions, data inconsistency, and unpredictable system behavior.

g.data-narration: With multi-core processors everywhere, concurrent programming is essential. Without synchronization, shared data access produces incorrect results.
The critical section is code that accesses shared resources and must not be executed by multiple processes simultaneously to prevent conflicts.
g.text: Code segment accessing shared resources that must not be concurrently accessed.
Only ONE process at a time

g.data-narration: Any valid critical section solution must satisfy: Mutual Exclusion (one at a time), Progress (only waiting processes decide), and Bounded Waiting (limited turns).
Hardware provides atomic instructions like test-and-set, swap, and compare-and-swap that cannot be interrupted, enabling lock implementation.
g.text: Key: These operations are ATOMIC - cannot be interrupted

g.data-narration: Mutex locks provide simple mutual exclusion with acquire/release. Semaphores use atomic wait() and signal() operations for resource counting.
g.text: Simple lock: acquire() before CS, release() after
Integer with atomic wait() and signal()

g.data-narration: Monitors combine shared data with procedures into one entity, providing automatic mutual exclusion - only one process active at a time.
g.text: Shared Data + Procedures - Automatic Mutual Exclusion

g.data-narration: Deadlock is circular waiting with permanent block (4 conditions). Starvation is indefinite waiting without deadlock - may eventually resolve.
Producer-Consumer: Producers add items to buffer, consumers remove them. Need empty/filled slot tracking plus mutex for mutual exclusion.
g.text: empty: tracks empty slots | full: tracks filled slots

g.data-narration: Readers-Writers: Multiple readers can access simultaneously, but writers need exclusive access. Balance between concurrency and data integrity.
g.text: Solution: track read_count, use mutex for read_count access, writer mutex for write access

g.data-narration: Dining Philosophers: 5 philosophers need 2 forks to eat, illustrating resource allocation and deadlock avoidance in concurrent systems.
Remember: CS requires 3 conditions, deadlock needs 4 conditions, semaphores use atomic wait/signal, monitors provide automatic exclusion.
g.text: Hardware atomicity is foundation of all sync

g.data-narration: For exams: memorize CS conditions, distinguish deadlock/starvation, know semaphore types, understand classical problems, practice pseudocode solutions.
g.text: Forgetting atomicity, busy-waiting waste, confusing semaphore types
